stem: A fair coin is flipped twice. What is the probability of getting heads then tails, in that order?
solutionStandard: The flips are independent: \(P(\text{H then T}) = \frac{1}{2}\times\frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{4}\).

stem: A standard die is rolled once. What is the probability of rolling a 2 or a 5?
solutionStandard: Rolling a 2 and rolling a 5 are mutually exclusive: \(P(2 \text{ or } 5) = 1/6+1/6=2/6=1/3\).

stem: A bag has 5 red and 5 blue marbles. Two are drawn without replacement. What is the probability both are red?
solutionStandard: First draw: \(5/10\). Second draw (one red removed): \(4/9\). \(P = \frac{5}{10}\times\frac{4}{9}=\frac{20}{90}=\frac{2}{9}\).

stem: A bag has 4 red and 4 blue marbles. Two are drawn.
solutionStandard: Without replacement: \(\frac{4}{8}\times\frac{3}{7}=\frac{12}{56}=\frac{3}{14}\approx0.214\). With replacement: \(\frac{4}{8}\times\frac{4}{8}=\frac{1}{4}=0.25\). Since \(0.25>0.214\), Quantity B is greater (removing a red marble without replacement makes the second draw less likely to be red).

stem: A fair coin is flipped 3 times. What is the probability of getting at least one heads? (Enter as a decimal.)
Which of the following pairs of events are independent? Select all that apply.

options.text: Flipping a coin twice (1st flip result, 2nd flip result)
Drawing two cards from a deck without replacement (1st card, 2nd card)
Drawing a card, replacing it, then drawing again

solutionStandard: (A), (C), and (D) are independent — each trial doesn't affect the next. (B) is dependent, since removing a card changes the composition of the deck for the second draw.

stem: A die is rolled twice. What is the probability of rolling at least one 6?
A bag has 3 red and 7 blue marbles. Two marbles are drawn without replacement. What is the probability that the two marbles are different colors? (Enter as a decimal.)
solutionStandard: \(P(\text{red then blue}) = \frac{3}{10}\times\frac{7}{9}=\frac{21}{90}\). \(P(\text{blue then red}) = \frac{7}{10}\times\frac{3}{9}=\frac{21}{90}\). Total: \(\frac{42}{90}=\frac{7}{15}\approx0.467\).

stem: Events \(A\) and \(B\) are not mutually exclusive, with \(P(A)=0.5\), \(P(B)=0.4\), and \(P(A \text{ and } B)=0.2\). What is \(P(A \text{ or } B)\)?
In a class, \(P(\text{likes math}) = 0.6\), \(P(\text{likes science}) = 0.5\), and \(P(\text{likes both}) = 0.3\).
solutionStandard: \(P(\text{math or science}) = P(\text{math}) + P(\text{science}) - P(\text{both}) = 0.6+0.5-0.3=0.8\). Since \(0.8 < 0.9\), Quantity B is greater.

stem: A fair coin is flipped {{n}} times. What is the probability of getting one particular exact sequence of results (for example, heads-tails-heads-...)? (Enter as a decimal.)
solutionStandard: The flips are independent, so multiply \(1/2\) by itself {{n}} times: \(P = {{1/(2**n)}}\).

stem: A standard die is rolled once. What is the probability of rolling a 1, 3, or 5?
solutionStandard: Rolling a 1, 3, or 5 are mutually exclusive outcomes: \(P = 1/6+1/6+1/6=3/6=1/2\).

stem: A bag has 6 red and 4 blue marbles. Two are drawn without replacement. What is the probability both are red?
solutionStandard: First draw: \(6/10\). Second draw (one red removed): \(5/9\). \(P = \frac{6}{10}\times\frac{5}{9}=\frac{30}{90}=\frac{1}{3}\).

stem: A bag has 3 red and 5 blue marbles. Two are drawn.
solutionStandard: Without replacement: \(\frac{5}{8}\times\frac{4}{7}=\frac{20}{56}=\frac{5}{14}\approx0.357\). With replacement: \(\frac{5}{8}\times\frac{5}{8}=\frac{25}{64}\approx0.391\). Quantity B is greater (removing a blue marble without replacement makes the second draw less likely to be blue).

stem: A fair coin is flipped {{n}} times. What is the probability of getting at least one heads? (Enter as a decimal.)
solutionStandard: \(P(\text{no heads})\) is \(1/2\) multiplied by itself {{n}} times. \(P(\text{at least one head}) = 1 - {{1/(2**n)}} = {{1-1/(2**n)}}\).

stem: Which of the following pairs of events are dependent (NOT independent)? Select all that apply.

options.text: Drawing 2 balls from an urn without replacement (1st ball, 2nd ball)
Flipping a coin, then rolling a die
Selecting a class president, then selecting a different student as vice president from those remaining

solutionStandard: (A) and (C) both remove an item from the pool before the next selection, so the second outcome's probabilities depend on the first — dependent events. (B) and (D) are separate trials that don't affect each other — independent.

stem: A die is rolled 3 times. What is the probability of rolling at least one 6?
A bag has 4 red and 6 blue marbles. Two marbles are drawn without replacement. What is the probability that the two marbles are different colors? (Enter as a decimal.)
solutionStandard: \(P(\text{red then blue}) = \frac{4}{10}\times\frac{6}{9}=\frac{24}{90}\). \(P(\text{blue then red}) = \frac{6}{10}\times\frac{4}{9}=\frac{24}{90}\). Total: \(\frac{48}{90}=\frac{8}{15}\approx0.533\).

stem: Events A and B are not mutually exclusive, with \(P(A)={{a/10}}\), \(P(B)={{b/10}}\), and \(P(A \text{ and } B)={{c/10}}\). What is \(P(A \text{ or } B)\)?
In a class, \(P(\text{likes math}) = 0.7\) and \(P(\text{likes science}) = 0.6\). The value of \(P(\text{likes both})\) is not given.
solutionStandard: \(P(\text{math or science}) = 0.7+0.6-P(\text{both}) = 1.3-P(\text{both})\). Since \(P(\text{both})\) can range from \(0.3\) (its minimum, from the inclusion-exclusion bound) up to \(0.6\) (its maximum, since it can't exceed either individual probability), \(P(\text{math or science})\) ranges from \(0.7\) to exactly \(1\). It could equal 1 or be less than 1, so the relationship cannot be determined.
